title: 帷幕
summary:
Poirot and Blackstone debate the presence of a killer at Styles Manor. Poirot asserts that the presence of "X" in the house implies a murder is imminent, using metaphors to explain that certain signs inevitably lead to certain conclusions. While Blackstone remains skeptical and suggests waiting, Poirot argues they must predict the outcome by identifying the potential victim who meets the specific conditions of X's criminal methods. Poirot instructs Blackstone to use his senses to observe the residents, though Blackstone resists spying due to his sense of gentlemanly honor.

The following day, Blackstone suggests involving Boyd Carrington, whom he believes is resourceful and intelligent enough to help. Poirot adamantly refuses, insulting Carrington as an arrogant, talkative man with a terrible memory. This prompts Blackstone to recall an instance where Carrington repeated a story to Poirot that Poirot had originally told him, confirming Carrington's poor memory.

Seeking clarity, Blackstone retreats to a dilapidated pavilion on a hill to analyze the residents' motives. He considers Colonel Lestrade but finds it unlikely he would commit a violent murder during a bridge game. He investigates financial motives, wondering if Mr. Norton, Miss Cole, or Colonel Lestrade might inherit from the wealthy Boyd Carrington. He also contemplates whether Dr. Franklin might poison his wife, Mrs. Franklin, and considers whether the attractive Judy might be implicated in a crime due to perceived romantic motives.
